text: Single incision laparoscopic colectomies have been performed and several reports have been published. These reports state that technical challenges make these a more difficult operation when compared to multiple port laparoscopic colectomy. Robotic assistance to seals with instrument crossing and harp control reassignment can facilitate the technical performance of the procedure. This is a case of a forty nine years old male admitted to the hospital with recurrent sigmoid diverticulitis with perforation. The CT scan that was performed confirmed the area of perforation in the sigmoid colon. The patient was treated with bowel rest, IV fluid and IV antibiotics with resolution of the acute event. An outpatient colonoscopy demonstrates sigmoid diverticulosis with mild inflammation and no other pathology.

A 4.5 cm incision is made 2 cm to the right of the umbilicus, where an applied medical gel port with a wound protector is placed. The da Vinci SI robot is docked, and robotic arms one and two are inserted into the peritoneal cavity through the single incision, crossing each other. The DaVinci monitor displays the reassignment of arm control, indicating the right hand now controls the robotic arm from the left side and the left hand controls the arm from the right.

The surgeon begins the operation by exposing and visualizing the vascular supply to the sigmoid colon for a medial to lateral dissection. The sigmoidal branches are carefully dissected free from surrounding tissue using monopolar endoshears. The exposed vessel is skeletonized, and a 5mm ligature device is used to seal and divide the largest vessels encountered. The dissection proceeds medially to laterally until reaching the opposite side of the mesosigmoid, creating a window in the mesentery. This window aids in retraction and facilitates traction and counter-traction. A longitudinal incision is made to accommodate the robotic arms along the craniocaudal axis of the operating field.

The surgeon continues the dissection around the rectosigmoid junction using a five millimeter ligature device to manage larger vessels. The rectosigmoid junction is prepared for an end-to-end anastomosis by ensuring proper serosal contraposition. A 60mm roticulating automatic endostapler is positioned at the most distal point with intact blood supply to divide the colon.

The surgeon uses a 5mm video scope with a 30-degree lens facing upward to provide a clear view of the surgical field, allowing ample room for robotic arm movements. A second reload of the stapler may be fired to complete the resection, or endoshears may be used to finish the procedure. The anatomy is visualized to localize the ureter, ensuring no limitation in the visual field.

The surgeon confirms adequate mobilization by checking if the descending colon reaches into the pelvis without tension. The colon is exteriorized through the single incision wound protector, and the specimen is transected. The anvil is placed in the distal descending colon extracorporeally. The gel port is reapplied on the wound protector, and the EEA stapled anastomosis is completed using regular laparoscopic instruments through the single port. The anastomotic donuts are checked on the back table, and the stapled line is tested underwater for leaks.

In conclusion, robotic-assisted single-incision laparoscopic semoecolectomy is a technically feasible procedure. The DaVinci robot greatly helped in overcoming the technical challenges of single-incision laparoscopic surgery due to internal instrument collision and external surgeon's arm interference. Thank you.
